edges.description: Loss of MMACHC lowers intracellular methylcobalamin available for methionine synthase.
Loss of MMACHC lowers intracellular adenosylcobalamin available for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase.
Reduced methylcobalamin supply lowers methionine synthase-side remethylation capacity.
Remethylation failure produces the defining sulfur-amino-acid abnormalities of cblC disease.
Reduced adenosylcobalamin supply lowers methylmalonyl-CoA mutase-side flux.
Impaired mutase-side flux produces the MMA branch of the combined disorder.
Impaired mutase-side flux can be detected on newborn screening as abnormal C3 or C3-ratio markers, although mild cases may require second-tier MMA testing.
intermediate_mechanisms: Propionyl-CoA and related acylcarnitine accumulation upstream of impaired methylmalonyl-CoA mutase flux

edges.description: Remethylation failure is measured clinically as increased plasma total homocysteine.
Failure to remethylate homocysteine depletes circulating methionine.
Hyperhomocysteinemia and low methionine are one component of the combined metabolic state that drives neuro-ocular, vascular, and renal morbidity.
edges.target: Combined methylmalonic acidemia and homocystinuria injury

edges.description: The mutase-side branch is measured clinically as elevated methylmalonic acid or methylmalonic aciduria.
Methylmalonic acid accumulation is the second component of the combined metabolic state that drives neuro-ocular, vascular, and renal morbidity.
edges.target: Combined methylmalonic acidemia and homocystinuria injury

edges.description: Early-onset combined biochemical disease is associated with persistent neurodevelopmental impairment.
edges.source: Combined methylmalonic acidemia and homocystinuria injury

edges.description: Neurologic injury in cblC includes seizures in pediatric cohorts.
edges.source: Combined methylmalonic acidemia and homocystinuria injury

edges.description: Neurologic injury in cblC includes hypotonia in pediatric cohorts.
edges.source: Combined methylmalonic acidemia and homocystinuria injury

edges.description: The combined methylmalonic acidemia-homocystinuria state can lead to infantile retinal degeneration and maculopathy.
edges.source: Combined methylmalonic acidemia and homocystinuria injury

edges.description: Children with methylmalonic acidemia and homocysteinemia can develop severe cardiovascular involvement, often including pulmonary hypertension.
edges.source: Combined methylmalonic acidemia and homocystinuria injury

edges.description: Renal cblC disease includes thrombotic microangiopathy and tubular injury in the setting of elevated serum and urine homocysteine.
intermediate_mechanisms: Homocysteine-associated renal endothelial injury and thrombotic microangiopathy
edges.source: Combined methylmalonic acidemia and homocystinuria injury
edges.target: Renal thrombotic microangiopathy with tubular injury

edges.description: Renal cblC injury can present clinically with proteinuria.
intermediate_mechanisms: Renal thrombotic microangiopathy and tubular injury
edges.source: Combined methylmalonic acidemia and homocystinuria injury

edges.description: Retinal degeneration and maculopathy provide the ocular mechanism for visual impairment in cblC.
Pulmonary hypertension is the most common cardiovascular complication reported in the cohort.
Elevated total homocysteine reports impaired methionine synthase-dependent remethylation.
Plasma total homocysteine tracks the hyperhomocysteinemia branch and treatment response.
Elevated methylmalonic acid reports impaired adenosylcobalamin-dependent methylmalonyl-CoA mutase metabolism.
Methylmalonic acid measurement tracks the MMA accumulation branch and response to hydroxocobalamin-based therapy.
Lower methionine reports failure of homocysteine remethylation to restore methionine.
Methionine recovery tracks improved remethylation balance during hydroxocobalamin-based treatment.
Abnormal C3 or C3 ratios are first-tier newborn-screening signals that correlate with the mutase-side branch, but second-tier MMA is more sensitive for mild cblC.

nodes.description: Betaine is used as adjunctive remethylation therapy together with hydroxocobalamin-based regimens, particularly in late-onset or severe disease.

evidence.reference_title: High-dose hydroxocobalamin achieves biochemical correction and improvement of neuropsychiatric deficits in adults with late onset cobalamin C deficiency.
evidence.snippet: A regimen of high-dose hydroxocobalamin (25 mg/day) together with betaine and folic acid resulted in rapid and sustainable biochemical correction, resolution of psychosis, improvement of neurological functions, and amelioration of brain and spinal cord lesions.

nodes.description: A subset of children with methylmalonic acidemia and homocysteinemia develop cardiovascular complications, most notably pulmonary hypertension.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and molecular spectrum of patients with methylmalonic acidemia and homocysteinemia complicated by cardiovascular manifestations.
evidence.snippet: Cardiovascular complications were found between the ages of 2 months and 12 years, with 9 patients having pulmonary hypertension, 7 having hypertension, and 5 having non-compaction of ventricular myocardium.

nodes.description: Concurrent methylmalonic acidemia and homocystinuria create the multisystem injury state responsible for the neurological, ocular, vascular, and renal complications of cblC disease.
nodes.id: Combined methylmalonic acidemia and homocystinuria injury

evidence.reference_title: Improved biochemical and neurodevelopmental profiles with high-dose hydroxocobalamin therapy in cobalamin C defect.
evidence.snippet: Cobalamin C (Cbl-C) defect causes methylmalonic acidemia, homocystinuria, intellectual disability and visual impairment, despite treatment adherence.

evidence.reference_title: High-dose hydroxocobalamin achieves biochemical correction and improvement of neuropsychiatric deficits in adults with late onset cobalamin C deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Individuals with late-onset cblC may be asymptomatic until manifesting neuropsychiatric symptoms, thromboembolic events, and renal disease.

nodes.description: Early-onset cblC can cause retinal degeneration and maculopathy within the first months of life, sometimes despite timely metabolic treatment and biochemical control.

evidence.reference_title: Retinal Changes in Early-Onset cblC Methylmalonic Acidemia Identified Through Expanded Newborn Screening: Highlights from a Case Study and Literature Review.
evidence.snippet: In cblC, retinal degeneration occurs in the first months of life despite timely treatment and adequate biochemical control, and it may manifest before any signs of visual deprivation appear.

nodes.description: Parenteral hydroxocobalamin is the central disease-modifying therapy for cblC deficiency. Higher-dose regimens are associated with better biochemical and neurodevelopmental outcomes in early-onset disease and with rapid metabolic correction in late-onset adults.

evidence.reference_title: Improved biochemical and neurodevelopmental profiles with high-dose hydroxocobalamin therapy in cobalamin C defect.
evidence.snippet: This study showed that "high-dose" OH-Cbl treatment in NBS-diagnosed children with severe early-onset Cbl-C defect led to a significant improvement in the metabolic profile and in neurocognitive outcome, compared to age-matched patients treated with a "low-dose" regimen.

evidence.reference_title: High-dose hydroxocobalamin achieves biochemical correction and improvement of neuropsychiatric deficits in adults with late onset cobalamin C deficiency.
evidence.snippet: A regimen of high-dose hydroxocobalamin (25 mg/day) together with betaine and folic acid resulted in rapid and sustainable biochemical correction, resolution of psychosis, improvement of neurological functions, and amelioration of brain and spinal cord lesions.

nodes.description: Remethylation failure elevates total homocysteine while depleting methionine, creating the homocystinuria branch of cblC disease.

evidence.reference_title: High-dose hydroxocobalamin achieves biochemical correction and improvement of neuropsychiatric deficits in adults with late onset cobalamin C deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Cobalamin C (cblC) deficiency is the most common inborn error of intracellular cobalamin metabolism caused by pathogenic variant(s) in MMACHC and manifests with methylmalonic acidemia, hyperhomocysteinemia, and hypomethioninemia with a variable age of presentation.

nodes.description: Generalized hypotonia is frequent in pediatric cohorts and accompanies the neurodevelopmental presentation of cblC disease.

evidence.reference_title: Evaluation of the clinical, biochemical, and molecular spectrum of Cobalamin C (CblC) defect in 33 patients from Pakistan.
evidence.snippet: The most common clinical features were cognitive impairment (n = 29), seizures (n = 23), motor developmental delay (n = 20), hypotonia (n = 17), and sparse/hypopigmented scalp hair (n = 16).

nodes.description: Disrupted cobalamin delivery to the methionine synthase arm produces remethylation failure with homocysteine accumulation and methionine depletion.

evidence.reference_title: Variable phenotypes and outcomes associated with the MMACHC c.1A>G variant in Chinese patients with combined methylmalonic acidemia and homocystinuria cblC type.
evidence.snippet: co-immunoprecipitation provided evidence for impaired interaction between the variant MMACHC and methionine synthase (MTR).

evidence.reference_title: Evaluation of the clinical, biochemical, and molecular spectrum of Cobalamin C (CblC) defect in 33 patients from Pakistan.
evidence.snippet: Biochemical hallmarks are elevated plasma total homocysteine (HCYs) and low methionine accompanied by methylmalonic aciduria.

nodes.description: Failure to supply adenosylcobalamin impairs the methylmalonyl-CoA mutase pathway and drives methylmalonic acid accumulation.

evidence.reference_title: The vitamin B12 processing enzyme, mmachc, is essential for zebrafish survival, growth and retinal morphology.
evidence.snippet: This recessive disorder is characterized by a failure to metabolize cobalamin into adenosyl- and methylcobalamin, which results in the biochemical perturbations of methylmalonic acidemia, hyperhomocysteinemia and hypomethioninemia caused by the impaired activity of the downstream enzymes, methylmalonyl-CoA mutase and methionine synthase.

nodes.description: Neurocognitive impairment remains a major morbidity burden in cblC, including in treated early-onset cohorts.

evidence.reference_title: Improved biochemical and neurodevelopmental profiles with high-dose hydroxocobalamin therapy in cobalamin C defect.
evidence.snippet: Cobalamin C (Cbl-C) defect causes methylmalonic acidemia, homocystinuria, intellectual disability and visual impairment, despite treatment adherence.

nodes.description: Biallelic MMACHC pathogenic variants impair intracellular processing of dietary cobalamin into its two metabolically active cofactor forms.

evidence.reference_title: Evaluation of the clinical, biochemical, and molecular spectrum of Cobalamin C (CblC) defect in 33 patients from Pakistan.
evidence.snippet: Cobalamin C is the most common inborn error of intracellular cobalamin metabolism caused by biallelic pathogenic variants in the MMACHC gene, leading to impaired conversion of dietary vitamin B12 into its two metabolically active forms, methylcobalamin and adenosylcobalamin.

evidence.reference_title: Missense mutations in MMACHC protein from cblC disease affect its conformational stability and vitamin B12-binding activity: The example of R161Q mutation.
evidence.snippet: MMACHC protein plays a crucial role in the metabolism of vitamin B12 (cobalamin, Cbl) by catalyzing its conversion into the active forms adenosylcobalamin (AdoCbl) and methylcobalamin (MeCbl), which serve as essential cofactors in key cellular reactions.

evidence.reference_title: Evaluation of the clinical, biochemical, and molecular spectrum of Cobalamin C (CblC) defect in 33 patients from Pakistan.
evidence.snippet: Cobalamin C is the most common inborn error of intracellular cobalamin metabolism caused by biallelic pathogenic variants in the MMACHC gene, leading to impaired conversion of dietary vitamin B12 into its two metabolically active forms, methylcobalamin and adenosylcobalamin.

evidence.reference_title: Variable phenotypes and outcomes associated with the MMACHC c.482G > A mutation: follow-up in a large CblC disease cohort.
evidence.snippet: Patients with this mutation tend to have a relatively better response to hydroxocobalamin, better metabolic control, and more favorable neurological outcomes.

evidence.snippet: Cobalamin C (cblC) deficiency is the most common inborn error of intracellular cobalamin metabolism caused by pathogenic variant(s) in MMACHC and manifests with methylmalonic acidemia, hyperhomocysteinemia, and hypomethioninemia with a variable age of presentation.

nodes.description: Impaired mutase-side cobalamin handling produces persistent methylmalonic acid elevation as the MMA branch of the combined metabolic phenotype.

evidence.snippet: Biochemical hallmarks are elevated plasma total homocysteine (HCYs) and low methionine accompanied by methylmalonic aciduria.

evidence.reference_title: Milder Form of Cobalamin C Disease May Be Missed by Newborn Screening: The Importance of Methylmalonic Acid Assessment.
evidence.snippet: All were recalled due to abnormalities in C3 or related ratios, along with elevated methylmalonic acid (MMA) levels.

readouts.interpretation: Abnormal C3 or C3 ratios are first-tier newborn-screening signals that correlate with the mutase-side branch, but second-tier MMA is more sensitive for mild cblC.

nodes.description: Renal involvement can present with proteinuria and hypertension, often reflecting underlying microangiopathic kidney injury.

evidence.reference_title: Late-onset renal TMA and tubular injury in cobalamin C disease: a report of three cases and literature review.
evidence.snippet: Here we described three teenage patients who presented with hematuria, proteinuria, and hypertension in clinical presentation.

nodes.description: Pulmonary hypertension is an important and potentially lethal vascular complication that can improve after metabolic therapy.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and molecular spectrum of patients with methylmalonic acidemia and homocysteinemia complicated by cardiovascular manifestations.
evidence.snippet: Cardiovascular complications were found between the ages of 2 months and 12 years, with 9 patients having pulmonary hypertension, 7 having hypertension, and 5 having non-compaction of ventricular myocardium.

nodes.id: Renal thrombotic microangiopathy with tubular injury

evidence.reference_title: Late-onset renal TMA and tubular injury in cobalamin C disease: a report of three cases and literature review.
evidence.snippet: They were diagnosed with renal involvement due to cblC deficiency after laboratory tests revealing elevated serum and urine homocysteine, renal biopsy showing TMA and tubular injury, along with genetic testing showing heterogeneous compound mutations in MMACHC.

nodes.description: Seizures are a common neurological manifestation, particularly in more severe pediatric disease.

evidence.snippet: The most common clinical features were cognitive impairment (n = 29), seizures (n = 23), motor developmental delay (n = 20), hypotonia (n = 17), and sparse/hypopigmented scalp hair (n = 16).

nodes.description: Infantile maculopathy and retinal degeneration can progress in the first months of life despite early treatment and biochemical control.

evidence.reference_title: Retinal Changes in Early-Onset cblC Methylmalonic Acidemia Identified Through Expanded Newborn Screening: Highlights from a Case Study and Literature Review.
evidence.snippet: In cblC, retinal degeneration occurs in the first months of life despite timely treatment and adequate biochemical control, and it may manifest before any signs of visual deprivation appear.

nodes.description: Recurrent MMACHC variant associated with later-onset, milder disease, lower mortality, and better neurological outcomes than comparison cohorts lacking this allele.

evidence.reference_title: Variable phenotypes and outcomes associated with the MMACHC c.482G > A mutation: follow-up in a large CblC disease cohort.
evidence.snippet: The c.482G > A variant in MMACHC is associated with late-onset and milder phenotypes of CblC disease.

evidence.reference_title: Missense mutations in MMACHC protein from cblC disease affect its conformational stability and vitamin B12-binding activity: The example of R161Q mutation.
evidence.snippet: Moreover, we show that the R161Q mutation decreases AdoCbl binding affinity and impairs the protein's ability to form homodimers, which are supposed to have a functional role.
